task: Examine and feel products to identify defects and variations from coloring and other processing standards.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single product unit by hand and eye. A trained inspector typically spends 30–90 seconds examining, touching, and judging one item against color/processing standards.
robot_success_rationale: Vision-based defect detection is mature, but combining tactile "feel" (texture, firmness, surface anomalies) with nuanced color judgment across varied product types strains current robot tactile sensing and generalization. High false-positive/negative risk on subtle defects.

task: Stamp, attach, or change price tags on merchandise, referring to price list.
human_time_rationale: One instance = stamping/attaching/changing one price tag on one item. A competent worker can locate the price, apply or swap a tag in ~1–2 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to handle small tags/stickers, read a price list, and precisely place labels on varied merchandise. Current humanoids struggle with small object manipulation, tag tools, and cross-referencing printed/digital lists reliably.

task: Spread soiled articles on work tables, and position stained portions over vacuum heads or on marble slabs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = spreading and positioning one soiled garment on a work table over a vacuum head or marble slab; a skilled worker takes ~1–3 minutes per article.
robot_success_rationale: Requires grasping limp, varied fabric articles, precisely aligning stained areas over specific targets (vacuum heads/slab). Cloth manipulation and precise stain-to-target alignment exceed current robot dexterity and perception reliability; unstructured garment handling remains a key frontier weakness.

task: Cut excess material or thread from finished products.
human_time_rationale: One instance = trimming excess thread/material from a single finished product. A skilled worker uses scissors or snips to trim all loose threads in roughly 1–3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise detection of fine threads against varied fabric backgrounds, dexterous pinching/cutting with scissors or snips, and avoiding cutting the product itself. Current robots lack the fine perception and dexterity for reliable thread trimming.

task: Ignite torches or start power supplies and strike arcs by touching electrodes to metals being welded, completing electrical circuits.
human_time_rationale: One instance = igniting a torch/power supply and striking an arc on a single workpiece. A skilled welder does this in ~1–3 minutes including setup checks.
robot_success_rationale: Striking a welding arc requires precise electrode-to-metal contact, fine dexterity, high-temperature tool handling, and real-time feedback. Current humanoids lack reliable fine manipulation and welding-specific perception for autonomous arc initiation.

task: Exchange paper currency for playing chips or coins.
human_time_rationale: One instance = exchanging one customer's cash for chips/coins. A cashier or cage worker counts bills, calculates denominations, and hands over chips/coins in ~1–3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires counting/handling flimsy paper currency, recognizing denominations, dispensing exact chip/coin values, and social interaction. Current robots lack reliable fine manipulation of paper bills and real-time currency recognition at acceptable accuracy.

task: Align layout marks with dies or blades.
human_time_rationale: One instance = aligning a single die or blade to layout marks. A skilled worker visually positions and registers the tool to marks in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise visual perception of fine layout marks and sub-millimeter alignment of sharp/heavy tooling. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, force feedback, and specialized perception needed for reliable, safe registration to tight tolerances.

task: Inspect parts for surface defects.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single part for surface defects. A competent worker visually examines all surfaces, taking ~2–4 minutes per part.
robot_success_rationale: Frontier humanoids can use cameras for basic visual inspection, but detecting subtle surface defects (scratches, pits, discoloration) across varied part geometries requires fine perception, controlled lighting, and reliable defect classification—still unreliable in unstructured settings.

task: Read electric, gas, water, or steam consumption meters and enter data in route books or hand-held computers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = reading a single utility meter and logging the value. A competent meter reader typically handles one meter in ~2–4 minutes including walking to it, reading the display, and entering data.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor/indoor navigation to varied meter locations, visually reading analog or digital gauges in variable lighting, and accurately entering data. Current humanoids struggle with unstructured outdoor terrain, precise gauge reading, and data-entry on handheld devices.

task: Examine parts to ensure metal shades and colors conform to specifications, using knowledge of metal heat-treating.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single part for color/shade conformance. A skilled heat-treat inspector visually assesses and judges a part in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Color/shade perception via cameras is feasible, but distinguishing subtle metal heat-treat color variations (e.g., temper colors, oxide layers) with sufficient accuracy and domain-specific judgment is well beyond current robot reliability. Lighting sensitivity and lack of heat-treat expertise further limit success.

task: Turn dials, handwheels, valves, or switches to regulate conveyor speeds, machine temperature, air pressure and circulation, and the flow or spray of coatings or paints.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting a single control (dial, valve, switch) to reach a target setting; a competent operator locates and turns it in ~1–5 minutes including reading gauges.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can turn knobs/valves in structured settings but struggle with varied control types (handwheels, small dials, toggle switches), reading analog gauges for feedback, and fine torque control needed to hit precise setpoints reliably.

task: Assess logs after cutting to ensure that the quality and length are correct.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single cut log. A worker visually checks dimensions and quality (knots, splits, defects) in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Vision-based length measurement and basic defect detection are feasible, but assessing wood quality nuances (grain, internal splits, moisture, subtle defects) requires expert tactile/visual judgment that current robots lack in unstructured outdoor/mill environments.

task: Press paper tape over joints to embed tape into sealing compound and to seal joints.
human_time_rationale: One instance = taping one drywall joint (~4–6 ft). A skilled taper applies compound, presses tape, and smooths it in about 2–4 minutes per joint.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force-controlled pressing along a linear joint, uniform embedding of tape into wet compound, and smooth tool manipulation over a vertical surface. Current humanoids lack the fine compliant control and surface-tracking needed for acceptable finish quality.

task: Place articles to be stripped into stripping tanks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = placing a single article (e.g., a metal part) into a stripping tank. A worker picks, positions, and submerges the item in ~3 minutes including handling and positioning.
robot_success_rationale: Pick-and-place of industrial parts is feasible, but chemical tank environments are hazardous and unstructured. Variable part shapes, wet/slippery surfaces, precise submersion placement, and safety constraints around corrosive chemicals reduce reliability significantly.

task: Inspect products to verify that they meet specifications and to determine whether machine adjustment is needed.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single product/batch at a workstation. A competent worker visually checks, measures, and judges conformance in ~2–5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Vision-based defect detection is mature, but combining perception, measurement tool use, judgment on spec conformance, AND deciding on machine adjustment requires multi-step generalization. Current humanoids lack reliable fine-measurement tool handling and contextual machine-adjustment decisions.

task: Open cabinet doors to cut multifilament threadlines away from guides, using scissors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = opening a cabinet, locating threadlines across guides, and cutting them free. A skilled textile worker completes this in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires opening a cabinet door (moderate), then identifying and cutting fine multifilament threadlines from small guides with scissors — extremely fine dexterity, precise perception of thin fibers, and tool-use in a confined space far exceeds 2026 robot capability.

task: Demagnetize mechanisms, using demagnetizing machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = demagnetizing a single mechanism. A worker picks up the part, passes it through/over the demagnetizer, and verifies. Typically takes 1–5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robot can grasp and move parts, but operating a specific demagnetizer (switches, positioning protocol, verification) requires tool-use generalization and precise part handling. No reliable feedback loop for magnetization verification.

task: Mix ingredients, such as liquor, soda, water, sugar, and bitters, to prepare cocktails and other drinks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one cocktail or drink order. A competent bartender measures, combines, shakes/stirs, and pours a drink in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise liquid measurement, pouring control, shaker handling, and generalization across many recipes. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, grip compliance, and fluid-handling precision needed for consistent, quality results.

task: Laminate teaching materials to increase their durability under repeated use.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one document/sheet. Inserting into pouch, feeding through laminator, trimming edges takes ~3 min for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise flat-surface alignment of thin paper into a laminating pouch, operating a specific appliance, and trimming. Fine paper handling and thin-sheet dexterity remain challenging; machine operation and sequencing add further difficulty for current humanoids.

task: Garnish and decorate dishes in preparation for serving.
human_time_rationale: One instance = garnishing and decorating one plated dish before service. A skilled line cook typically takes 1–5 minutes per plate depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity (placing micro-herbs, sauce drizzles, precise arrangement), aesthetic judgment, and delicate force control. Current humanoids lack the precision, visual creativity, and gentle touch needed for acceptable plating quality.

task: Examine and inspect stock items for wear or defects, reporting any damage to supervisors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single stock item. A competent worker visually and physically checks one item for wear/defects and notes findings in ~3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Vision-based defect detection is improving but generalizing across varied item types, subtle wear patterns, and unstructured stock environments is unreliable. Reporting to supervisors adds communication/social complexity beyond current robot capability.

task: Weigh or measure specified amounts of ingredients or materials for processing, using devices such as scales and calipers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring a single specified ingredient/material. A competent worker reads the spec, selects the container, places it on a scale or uses calipers, and records or transfers the result in ~3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can pick objects and read digital scales via vision, but reliably scooping/pouring precise amounts, zeroing a tare, and handling varied containers and materials (powders, liquids, small parts) with caliper-level dexterity remains inconsistent on frontier humanoids.

task: Inspect surface to be covered to ensure that it is firm and dry.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one surface (e.g., a wall or floor section) before covering. A competent worker visually checks and touches the surface in ~3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can visually scan surfaces and detect obvious wetness or instability, but reliably assessing firmness and subtle moisture (e.g., damp drywall, soft substrate) requires nuanced tactile and multimodal sensing beyond current frontier robot capability.

task: Hold cut or uneven edges of glass against automated abrasive belts to shape or smooth edges.
human_time_rationale: One instance = grinding/smoothing the edges of a single glass piece. A skilled worker typically spends ~2–4 minutes per piece feeding and guiding it against the abrasive belt.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force-controlled manipulation of a fragile, irregular glass piece against a moving belt. Glass is brittle and hard to grip safely; edge irregularities demand adaptive tactile feedback current robots lack. High breakage risk.

task: Weigh meats and tag containers for weight and contents.
human_time_rationale: One instance = weighing a single meat container, reading the scale, and attaching a label with weight/contents. A competent worker does this in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires picking up varied meat containers, placing on scale, reading the display, printing/writing a label, and affixing it accurately. Multi-step perception, fine dexterity, and tool-use chain is beyond reliable 2026 robot capability in unstructured settings.
